coredata.dc:title: Novel visible-light-excited afterglow rose-bengal-derived carbon dots and their applications
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Mingming
Wang, Peipei
Cao, Lei
Dong, Wen-Fei
Li, Li
Yan, Ruhong
coredata.dc:description: Compared to ultraviolet light, visible light is much more accessible and less damaging. However, afterglow materials that are excited by visible light remain a challenging issue. In this study, visible-light-excited phosphorescence combined with thermally activated delayed fluorescence were achieved by embedding Rose bengal (R) CDs (R-CDs) with a high quantum yield in a boric acid (BA) matrix. The R-CDs were prepared by thermally cleaving Rose bengal in an aqueous solution. At room temperature, the R-CDs@BA composite exhibited a dual-emission afterglow spectrum with an afterglow lifetime of 583 ms at an emission wavelength of 450 nm. The afterglow time was visible to the naked eye for up to 5 s, and the photoluminescence quantum yield reached 18.09%. The quantum yield of phosphorescence is 0.6%. Consequently, the R-CDs@BA composite materials were successfully employed in anti-counterfeiting, graphic security and white led emitting diodes. In summary, R-CDs@BA hold great promises for the application in different fields. New afterglow materials and applications that are stimulated by visible light may be made possible as a result of these discoveries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rose bengal
Visible light
Afterglow
Carbon dots
WLED
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application in white light emitting diodes (WLEDs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of R-CDs. (b) Size distribution of R-CDs. (c) XRD patterns of Rose bengal and R-CDs. (d) Absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of R-CDs in aqueous solution. (e) Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs in aqueous solution under excitation at different wavelengths. (f) Fluorescence lifetime decay of R-CDs.

(a) Afterglow images of R-CDs@BA under different excitation lights. (b) PL emission spectra of R-CDs@BA solid. (c) Afterglow emission spectra of R-CDs@BA solid. (d) Afterglow emission spectra of R-CDs@BA solid. (e) Schematic of the emission processes of the R-CDs@BA composite.
(a) XRD patterns of R-CDs@BA. (b) FTIR spectra of R-CDs@BA.
(a) Image of a UV-pumped WLED lamp and the EL spectrum and (b) CIE color coordinates of the WLED lamp.
(a) Multiple anti-counterfeiting and information encryption applications of R-CDs@BA. (b–c) Graphic security application of R-CDs@BA. (d) R-CDs@BA for barcode anti-counterfeiting applications.
Schematic of the synthetic pathway of R-CDs@BA

head.ce:title: Novel visible-light-excited afterglow rose-bengal-derived carbon dots and their applications

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Clinical Laboratory, Suzhou Science & Technology Town Hospital, Gusu School, Nanjing Medical University, Suzhou 215153, People's Republic of China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Compared to ultraviolet light, visible light is much more accessible and less damaging. However, afterglow materials that are excited by visible light remain a challenging issue. In this study, visible-light-excited phosphorescence combined with thermally activated delayed fluorescence were achieved by embedding Rose bengal (R) CDs (R-CDs) with a high quantum yield in a boric acid (BA) matrix. The R-CDs were prepared by thermally cleaving Rose bengal in an aqueous solution. At room temperature, the R-CDs@BA composite exhibited a dual-emission afterglow spectrum with an afterglow lifetime of 583 ms at an emission wavelength of 450 nm. The afterglow time was visible to the naked eye for up to 5 s, and the photoluminescence quantum yield reached 18.09%. The quantum yield of phosphorescence is 0.6%. Consequently, the R-CDs@BA composite materials were successfully employed in anti-counterfeiting, graphic security and white led emitting diodes. In summary, R-CDs@BA hold great promises for the application in different fields. New afterglow materials and applications that are stimulated by visible light may be made possible as a result of these discoveries.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), novel zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, have been gaining considerable attention due to their unique properties such as low toxicity, good biocompatibility, easy production, and exceptional optical properties. Several studies have reported their promising potential in various applications including [1–3] bioimaging systems [4,5], sensors [6,7], optical devices [8,9], safe data encryption devices [10–12]. In fact, the optical properties of CDs, in particular their fast fluorescence, have been widely studied [13–15].

However, there are only a few studies on the afterglow of CDs, such as through the investigation of their phosphorescence or thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) [16]. Phosphorescence is the process by which light is emitted from triplet excited states at relatively low emission rates. Consequently, fluorescence materials can emit light for a long time ranging from several milliseconds to even hours. In general, the phosphorescence process is initiated by a light-excited single-line exciton in its lowest single-line state (S1), which undergoes inter-system crossing (ISC), followed by radiative relaxation to its ground state (S0).

In contrast, TADF is generated by collecting triplet excited states for reverse ISC (RISC) from the T1 to S1 excited states [17]. Due to the unique characteristics of the afterglow, TADF materials have been employed in different fields, including optics, sensing, bioimaging, and information protection [18]. Although traditional room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials, which include organometallic complexes and metal-doped inorganic materials, are effective phosphorescent materials, they have high fabrication cost, high cytotoxicity, and low stability [19–21]. Furthermore, although organic phosphorescence materials have been developing rapidly, their toxicity, high fabrication cost, weak ISC, and high rate of nonradiative deactivation have restricted their application [22].

Currently, CDs are considered the new type of luminescent materials because they offer extraordinary benefits for the development of novel metal-free phosphorescence materials owing to their high optical properties, relative non-toxicity, convenient preparation, and low fabrication cost. RTP CDs can be achieved via two main approaches. The first approach involves the introduction of heteroatoms [23–25], which promote an effective spin-orbit coupling and result in low splitting energy between singlets and triplets [10]. The second approach involves embedding CDs in various matrices including polyvinyl alcohol [26,27], aluminum sulfate [28], silica gel [29,30], urea [31,32], and boric acid (BA) [33,34]. These matrices can contribute to rigid conditions, which can effectively fix emissive species and stabilize the triplet states.

Compared to ultraviolet light, visible light is more beneficial as an excitation source because of its lower phototoxicity, better penetration for bioanalysis and bioimaging, and easier access in practical applications [35,36]. Unfortunately, development of afterglow materials obtained by visible-light excitation is a significant challenge. To the best of our knowledge, only a few studies have reported CDs that exhibit phosphorescence upon by visible light exposure.

In this study, R-CDs dispersed in a BA matrix were synthesized to induce visible-light-excited thermally activated delayed fluorescence coupled with phosphorescence, which is one of the few reports devoted to CDs-based materials producing visible-light (Scheme 1 ) [16]. The R-CDs @BA composite exhibited a dual-emission afterglow spectrum with an afterglow lifetime of 583 ms at an emission wavelength of 450 nm at room temperature. The afterglow time was visible to the naked eye for up to 5 s, and the photoluminescence quantum yield reached 18.09%. The quantum yield of phosphorescence is 0.6%. Besides，the R-CDs@BA composite materials were successfully employed in anti-counterfeiting, graphic security and white led emitting diodes.
Rose bengal (R) and BA were purchased from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. (China). Ultrapure water was used as the solvent for all experiments.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were collected at 100 kV using a TECNAI G2 microscope (FEI, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a VERTEX 70 FT-IR spectrometer, (Bruker, Germany). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were acquired using Rigaku Minister equipment (Tokyo, Japan). The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Hitachi UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence spectra were obtained using an F97Pro FL spectrophotometer coupled with a 1.0 cm quartz cell (Lengguang Technology, Shanghai, China). An FLS-1000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, UK) was used to analyze the fluorescence and phosphorescence lifetimes at room temperature.

In general, Rose bengal sodium salt (90 mg) was dissolved in ultrapure water (15 mL) and the solution was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene lined reaction bottle (50 mL). The mixture was left to react at 180 °C in a drying oven for 8 h. The solution was then cooled to room temperature, filtered using a centrifuge and a needle pore filter, and finally freeze-dried to obtain the R-CDs as solid pellets.

R-CDs with different masses (1 mg, 2 mg, 3 mg, and 4 mg) were respectively added into small beakers filled with ultrapure water (20 mL). BA (3 g) was then added into each beaker, covered with a tin foil, and placed in a drying oven for at 180 °C for 5 h. After the reaction was completed, the samples were ground into powders to obtain R-CDs1mg@BA, R-CDs2mg@BA, R-CDs3mg@BA, and R-CDs4mg@BA, respectively.

R-CDs were synthesized by a novel method involving the hydrothermal pyrolysis of Rose bengal at 180 °C for 8 h in an aqueous solution. The TEM images of the R-CDs revealed particles with a spherical morphology and an average size of 5.32 nm (Fig. 1 a). Furthermore, the R-CDs had a lattice fringe with an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm close to the (100) diffraction facet of graphite carbon. The size distribution of the R-CDs is shown in Fig. 1b; the results indicated that the diameters of R-CDs ranged from 3.0 to 7.0 nm. The XRD patterns of the R-CDs showed two distinct peaks at 31.5° and 45° (Fig. 1c) [29], which were significantly different from those of R's crystalline structure. The FTIR spectra allowed the identification of the functional groups present on the surface of the R-CDs (Fig. S1a). The broad absorption peak observed at 3400 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H, while the absorption maxima at 1735 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1 were attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of COO–and C = C, respectively. The absorption peaks at 1425 cm−1 and 1145 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of C–H and the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of C–O–C, respectively. Finally, the absorption maxima at 637 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of C–I [33].

A strong and sharp peak at 226 nm and a faint band at 507 nm appeared in the absorption spectrum of the R-CDs, which were attributed to the π−π transition of the C=C transition and the n−π * transition of C=O, respectively. The R-CDs exhibited a broad characteristic absorption band at 510 nm in their excitation spectrum, which almost completely overlapped with the corresponding bands in the absorption spectrum (Fig. 1d), indicating the absence of nonradiative transition. In general, the absorption spectra of R-CDs closely resembled that of R, except that the distinctive absorption bands were blue-shifted (Fig. S1b). Under different excitation conditions, fluorescence emission peaks were all centered at 530 nm (Fig. 1e), indicating an excitation-independent behavior. However, the fluorescence peak of the R-CDs was blue-shifted by 35 nm in relation to that of Rose bengal (Fig. S1c). Based on these results, it was concluded that the fluorescence of the R-CDs was due to a molecular state, which was generated by fluorophores from Rose bengal attached to the graphitic carbon core. Furthermore, the absolute quantum yield of the R-CDs was 39.01%, and their fluorescence lifetime was 4.19 ns (Fig. 1f, Table S1). Considering R-CDs possess a high quantum yield in aqueous solution, they are capable of being used as solid optical materials with excellent performance.

The aggregation that occurred in the R-CDs induced quenching, which prevented solid fluorescence and afterglow. Interestingly, when R-CDs are dissolved in boric acid (BA) solution, the R-CDs@BAs also exhibited a bright afterglow as well as solid fluorescence under a different light. Furthermore, a bright afterglow was observed when the R-CDs@BA composite was exposed to visible light (flashlight) and 450 nm excitation light, which persisted for up to approximately 5 s after the removal of the UV light (Fig. 2 a).

To achieve an optimum afterglow effect for the R-CDs@BA composites, the ratio of R-CDs to BA was optimized. Fig. 2b shows the solid fluorescence spectra of the R-CDs1mg@BA composite excited at various wavelengths. Furthermore, the solid fluorescence spectra of the R-CDs exhibited an excitation-independent behavior, similar to that of aqueous solutions. Upon excitation at different wavelengths, all emission spectra revealed an excitation peak at 501 nm, and their maximum emission occurred under excitation at a wavelength of 450 nm, which was blue-shifted by approximately 29 nm as compared to the maximum emission of R-CDs in aqueous solution. In addition, a shoulder emission was observed at approximately 500 nm which may be attributed to delayed fluorescence [35].

The afterglow emission spectra were examined under stimulation at wavelengths ranging between 254 nm and 480 nm (Fig. 2c). Similar to the fluorescence spectra, the afterglow emission spectra also demonstrated excitation-independent properties. Based on the excitation spectra, the maximum afterglow was observed at 450 nm for the R-CDs1mg@BA composite. Two peaks were observed in the afterglow emission spectrum: a broad band near 500 nm and a shoulder peak near 586 nm. Considering that the emission spectra of afterglow emission almost entirely overlapped with those of solid fluorescence, it was deduced that the 490 nm emission was associated with delayed fluorescence. In addition, a band of afterglow emission at 596 nm, which was longer than the maximum emission wavelength of solid fluorescence, can be attributed to RTP. The fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of R-CDs2mg@BA, R-CDs3mg@BA, and R-CDs4mg@BA were recorded; the results are shown in Figs. S2, S3, and S4, respectively, which were almost the same as the emission diagram of R-CDs1mg@BA. The afterglow lifetime of the prepared R-CDs1mg@BA composite was at 583 ms at room temperature (Fig. 2d) with a photoluminescence quantum yield of 18.09% under the optimized conditions (ratio of R-CDs to BA (mg/g) of 1/3, 180 °C, and 5 h). By calculating the spectral integrated area, the quantum yield of phosphorescence is 0.6% [2,37]. The synthesized afterglow material had a significant advantage in its lifetime in relation to the results of other studies (Table S2). The lifetimes of R-CDs2mg@BA, R-CDs3mg@BA, and R-CDs4mg@BA were calculated to be 582, 400, and 558 ms (Table S3), respectively, and the life decay curves are shown in Figs. S2, S3, and S4, respectively.

When R-CDs are scattered in the BA matrix, the boron atoms with vacant p orbitals attract π electrons to create a p–π conjugate system, thus lowering the energy level of the R-CDs lowest empty orbital level. BA was essential for the formation of afterglow, which resulted in the formation of C–B bonds when paired with R-CDs. The RTP emission may have been facilitated by the covalent bonds and nanoconfined space of BA, which may have prevented the excited triplet excitons from being extinguished. The variation of singlet-triplet splitting (ΔEST) between the lowest single (S1) and triplet (T1) states was 0.39 eV (equation (1)), which is a modest value that enables effective ISC to proceed (Fig. 2e, Table S4).
(1) EST = h/k*C/λ = 1240/λ
Furthermore, ΔEST may be efficiently decreased, hence facilitating the RISC between T1 and S1 [34,38].

XRD and FTIR analyses were performed to further determine the structure of the R-CDs@BA composites. The four different R-CDs@BA showed almost identical XRD patterns. Three distinct peaks were observed at 2ϴ = 11°, 27.80°, and 40.18°, which were attributed to the characteristic peaks of BA (Fig. 3 a) [38]. As shown in Fig. 3b, the absorption maxima at 3222 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H, and the faint stretching vibration of C≡C was responsible for the broad absorption peak observed at 2256 cm−1. The characteristic peaks at 1460 cm−1 and 1196 cm−1 were attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of C–H and the stretching vibrations of C–O–C, respectively. The FT-IR spectral absorption bands of the R-CDs@BA composites with varying amounts of R-CDs were almost identical, demonstrating that the R-CDs were disseminated throughout the matrix rather than adhering to the surface [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in white light emitting diodes (WLEDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The R-CDs@BA material has a great potential for application in WLEDs due to their efficient dual delayed fluorescence and phosphorescence in addition to their economical and environmental friendly preparation [40]. UV-pumped WLEDs were successfully prepared by mixing R-CDs@BA powder material with epoxy resin AB glue and were then placed on a chip with a 395 nm UV emission. The LED chip operated at a voltage of 3 V and a current of 150 mA. At a voltage of 3 V, the 395-nm UV-LED produced efficient white emission (Fig. 4 a). Furthermore, the LED exhibited light-yellow phosphorescence upon the removal of the electrical stimulation. As shown in Fig. 4a, the electroluminescence (EL) spectrum of the effective single-component WLED based on the R-CDs@BA covered a wide spectral area ranging from 380 to 780 nm. The UV-LED emitting at 395 nm produced white light with CIE color coordinates (0.31, 0.29) (Fig. 4b). The efficiency of WLED is 330 mW/W according to the optical energy/electrical input energy [29].

Due to the superior visible-light excitation behavior of the R-CDs@BA composites under flashlight illumination, information encryption, double anti-counterfeiting, and image protection were effectively implemented. As shown in Fig. 5 a, a number “8″ mold with the R-CDs@BA material and other common materials was prepared and exposed to a 365 nm UV radiation. When the lamp was turned off, only number “7″ was visible. Interestingly, number “8″ was almost indistinguishable under the LED flashlight light source, yet the number “3″ appeared after the light was turned off. In addition, the numbers were irradiated with a 488 nm excitation light, and only number “7″ was visible after the light was turned off.

Fig. 5b and c illustrate a typical instance of a triple-modal switching encryption produced by filling the molds with the R-CDs@BA powder. In addition, the material was successfully applied for image protection. Under 365 nm UV light, the pylon pattern and five petals were visible, and a clear afterglow pattern also appeared after the light was turned off. When exposed to visible light (flashlight) or 488 nm laser light, there was almost no difference in the pattern. Surprisingly, after the light source was turned off, the pattern significantly changed with only a part of the pattern appearing in the pylon, and only three petals being visible. These results indicated the great potential of R-CDs@BA in image protection. Fig. 5d illustrates the barcode anti-counterfeiting applications of R-CDs@BA. First, a random barcode was generated on the website and a barcode pattern with R-CDs@BA and other interfering materials was created. The wrong barcode was observed under visible torch light, 450 nm blue light, and 365 nm UV light. Interestingly, when the excitation light was turned off, the correct barcode pattern could be observed. These findings demonstrated the practical application of R-CDs@BA in double anti-counterfeiting and data-encryption protection.

A simple and economical method to achieve light afterglow emissions from CDs is reported where the afterglow phenomenon can be realized using a variety of excitation sources. The hydrothermal degradation of Rose bengal in an aqueous solution resulted in CDs with high quantum rates that radiated orange-red light. The R-CDs@BA composite exhibited a dual-emission afterglow spectrum with an afterglow lifetime of 583 ms at an emission wavelength of 450 nm at room temperature, and the afterglow time was visible to the naked eye for up to 5 s. The photoluminescence quantum yield reached 18.09%. The R-CDs@BA displayed visible-light-excited afterglow due to the presence of glassy boron oxides with empty p orbitals, which greatly contributed to the occurrence of ISC as well as stabilization of the triplet state. The unique afterglow characteristics of this material allowed its application in information encryption devices, double security systems, and WLEDs.

MingMing Cheng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Peipei Wang: Supervision, Software, Validation, Writing - review & editing. Lei Cao: Methodology, Data curation. Wen-Fei Dong: Methodology, Data curation, Validation. Li: Software, Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Ruhong Yan: Software, Data curation, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the National Key R&D Program of China (Grand No. 2020YFC2004600), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grand No. 91959112, 81902166, 62027825, and 82172077), the Science Foundation of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (No. 2020SYHZ0041), the Instrument Developing Project of Chinese Academy of Sciences (YJKYYQ20200038), the Youth Innovation Promotion Association of CAS (2022326), and the Suzhou Science and Technology Bureau (SKY2021032).

J. Mater. Chem. C
